coredata.dc:title: SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters: A fluorescence probe for the mutual non-interference detection of uric acid and l-cysteine under alkaline conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Yonggang
Shao, Fan
Guo, Kaixi
Zhuo, Hanhan
Wang, Yuan
Xie, Xiaowei
Tao, Yongxin
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescence probes based on quantum dots (QDs) have been widely used in the detection of uric acid (UA), but other biomolecules such as biothiols, ascorbic acid and dopamine have interference with the detection result due to their similar chemical properties. Herein, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters were prepared via coordination-drivenself-assembly between Cu2-β-CD and –NH2 on the surface of SiQDs, and UA could attenuate the fluorescence of the nanoclusters under strong alkali conditions, while l-cysteine (l-Cys) had no effect. Under the optimum conditions, the decreased fluorescence intensity is linearly proportional to the concentration of UA in the range of 0.025–0.15 mM with a detection limit of 4.9*10-3 mM. Furthermore, under neutral or weak base conditions, l-Cys could improve the fluorescence of the nanocluster, but UA had no effect, which could also be used to detect l-Cys. This work provides a sensitive, selective and convenient fluorescence method for UA and l-Cys detection in the present of another interfering biomolecule.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
β-Cyclodextrin
Nanoclusters
Uric acid detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of SiQDs and SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters
Response of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters to UA
Fluorescence attenuation mechanism of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters in the present of UA at pH = 13
Fluorescence detection of UA based on SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at pH 13
Selectivity of the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters for UA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the prepared SiQDs (a) and SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters (b).

(a) Effect of the separate addition of l-Cys and UA on fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at different pH. The initial concentrations of SiQDs diluent added into the detection solution of l-Cys and UA are 0.4 and 0.7 g/L, respectively. The concentrations of l-Cys and UA in the detection solution are 0.3 and 0.5 mM, respectively. (b) Effect of l-Cys on the detection system of UA at pH 13. The initial concentration of SiQDs diluent added into the detection solution is 0.7 g/L. The concentrations of l-Cys and UA in the detection solution are 1.0 and 0.5 mM, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters after adding UA at pH 13. The initial concentration of SiQDs diluent added into the detection solution is 0.7 g/L. The concentration of UA in the detection solution is 0.5 mM. (b) Schematic illustration after adding UA into SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters solution at pH 13.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters with different concentrations of UA at pH 13. The initial concentration of SiQDs diluent added into the detection solution is 0.7 g/L. (b) The plot of the values for (I0-I)/I0 versus concentration of UA in the range of 0–0.15 mM, where I and I0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the solution in the presence and absence of UA, respectively.

The fluorescent intensities of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters for UA detection in the present of other interfering species. The initial concentration of SiQDs diluent added into the detection solution of UA is 0.7 g/L. The concentrations of UA and interfering species are 0.5 mM and 1.0 mM, respectively, excepting DA concentration is 0.25 mM. The pH of the mixed solution was controlled at 13 by 1 mol/L NaOH solution.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the UA detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aLOD = limit of detection; bRef. = reference; cInterfering species: Yes = Interference; No = No interference; - = Not tested.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for determination of UA in human urine sample.

head.ce:title: SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters: A fluorescence probe for the mutual non-interference detection of uric acid and l-cysteine under alkaline conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University, of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University, of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Changzhou University, 21 Gehu Middle Road, Wujin District, Changzhou City, Jiangsu Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: l-Cysteine can enhance the fluorescence of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at pH 8; uric acid can attenuate their fluorescence at pH 13. SiQDs = silicon quantum dots; Cu2-β-CD = Cu-modified-β-cyclodextrin; l-Cys = l-cysteine.

The fluorescence probes based on quantum dots (QDs) have been widely used in the detection of uric acid (UA), but other biomolecules such as biothiols, ascorbic acid and dopamine have interference with the detection result due to their similar chemical properties. Herein, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters were prepared via coordination-drivenself-assembly between Cu2-β-CD and –NH2 on the surface of SiQDs, and UA could attenuate the fluorescence of the nanoclusters under strong alkali conditions, while l-cysteine (l-Cys) had no effect. Under the optimum conditions, the decreased fluorescence intensity is linearly proportional to the concentration of UA in the range of 0.025–0.15 mM with a detection limit of 4.9*10-3 mM. Furthermore, under neutral or weak base conditions, l-Cys could improve the fluorescence of the nanocluster, but UA had no effect, which could also be used to detect l-Cys. This work provides a sensitive, selective and convenient fluorescence method for UA and l-Cys detection in the present of another interfering biomolecule.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is the end product of purine metabolite [1], and its concentration in serum and urine can reflect human health condition [2]. The quantitative determination of UA is of vital importance for clinical diagnosis and disease research, therefore, a variety of analytical methods including high performance liquid chromatography [3], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [4], electrochemistry [5], fluorescence [6] have been developed for this purpose. Compared with the above methods, fluorescence analysis has the characteristics of simple operation, low cost, rapid response and high sensitivity [7,8], and widely used in the detection of UA [9], ascorbic acid [10], l-cysteine [11], antibiotic [12], environmental pollutions [13], etc. Fluorescent probes based on gold/silver nanoclusters [14,15], lanthanide coordination polymers or nanoparticles [16,17], metal–organic frameworks [18], organic fluorescent compound [19,20] and quantum dots (QDs) [21] have been designed to determine UA.

QDs are the preferred fluorescent material for constructing fluorescent probes because of their good optical stability, broad absorption spectra, narrow and tunable emission spectra [22,23]. The QDs-based probes for UA detection can be divided into enzyme and enzyme-free methods [24]. Enzymatic-fluorometric determination is mostly achieved by measuring H2O2, which is generated from the oxidation of UA catalyzed by enzyme/peroxidase-like [25]. Enzyme-free fluorescence detection is mainly realized in two ways. One is to monitor the change of fluorescence intensity, which is caused by static quenching and inner filter effect due to the electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions between UA and –COOH, –NH2, –OH on the surface of QDs [26–28]. For example, UA was able to quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs coated with 2-mercaptoethylamine, which was used to determine UA in the biological samples [29]. UA could form complex with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-capped core–shell ZnS:Cu/ZnS QDs, which resulted in a passivation shell around the nanocrystal. As the surface defects of QDs were removed, the luminescent efficiency was dramatically improved [30]. Another is to eliminate the factors leading to QDs fluorescence quenching and restore their fluorescence using the reducibility or coordination ability of UA. For example, Amjadi et al [31]. reported sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs)-MnO2 nanosheets for detection of UA, MnO2 nanosheets could quench the fluorescence of S,N-CDs by the inner filter effect, and the fluorescence of S,N-CDs was partially recovered as a result of a partial reduction of MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ by UA. Wu et al [32]. found that the fluorescence of the C-dots prepared from polyethylenimine (PEI) was quenched by periodate because amino (CNH2) on PEI C-dots was oxidized to nitrone (CN+-O-). UA could reduce nitrone to hydroxylamine (CNOH), which recovered the fluorescence of PEI C-dots. Hg(II) could coordinate with the amino and CO on the silicon-nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (Si/N-CDs), resulting in the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. UA could recover the fluorescence of the Si/N-CDs-Hg(II) complex by stronger coordination between UA with Hg(II) [33]. In addition, Ce(IV) could oxidize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to produce a relatively intense chemiluminescence (CL) emission, and the competition of UA with GQDs for Ce(IV) resulted in a diminishing CL effect, which was used to the determination of UA [34].

So far, much attention has been paid to improve the sensitivity of QDs fluorescent probes for UA detection. However, the research on improving the selectivity of fluorescent probes is still not thorough. The presence of other biomolecules (ascorbate, bilirubin) has interfered with the enzymatic-fluorometric determination for UA [25,35]. Enzyme-free fluorescence probes, whether the direct fluorescence quenching of QDs by electrostatic attraction or hydrogen bond [36,37] or the indirect fluorescence recovery of QDs by eliminating the quenching agent or quenching group [38], are easily interfered by other biological analytes such as biothiols, ascorbic acid and dopamine due to their similar reducibility and coordination as UA, as well as electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonds with functional group on the surface of QDs [31,33,39]. Therefore, it is still very challenging to detect UA using the QDs-based enzyme-free fluorescence probes in the presence of the above other biomolecules.

β-cyclodextrin could bind a variety of guest molecules inside its hydrophobic cavity to form a host–guest inclusion complex and was widely used to modify fluorescent probes to improve their selectivity [40,41]. Recently, Dalal et al [42]. synthesized water-soluble β-cyclodextrin-capped ZnO-doped carbon dot (ZnO-CD), which was utilized as selective detection of dopamine (DA) in the presence of UA, cysteine, ascorbic acid, etc. β-cyclodextrin was also used to help construct capsule-like Pb(II)-based metal–organic nanotube for fluorescence detection of UA with high selectivity [43]. Although these fluorescent probes have excellent selectivity and sensitivity, they can only respond to a single analyte. So far, there are few reports on the QDs-based fluorecence probe for UA detection that are not interfered by other biomolecules and can detect two biomolecules. Herein, we built a novel fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots/Cu-modified-β-cyclodextrin (SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD) nanoclusters via coordination-drivenself-assembly between Cu2-β-CD and –NH2 on the surface of SiQDs. Under strong alkaline conditions, allantoin decomposed from UA could form host–guest inclusion complex with Cu2-β-CD and quench the fluorescence of nanoclusters in the present of l-cysteine (l-Cys). Surprisingly, under neutral or weak base conditions, l-Cys could improve the fluorescence of the nanocluster, while UA has no effect, so as to achieve the mutual non-interference detection of uric acid and l-Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SiQDs and SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectrum was used to investigate the surface functional groups of SiQDs (Fig. S1). The characteristic peaks at 3357 and 3250 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the NH in the –NH2 and OH groups [44]. The peaks at 2931, 1487, 1338 cm−1 are related to CH unsaturated stretching vibration, CH bending vibration and CN stretching vibration [45,46], respectively. And the band at 1595 cm−1 corresponds to the NH bending vibration in the –NH2 [47]. The peaks at 757 and 692 cm−1 are attributed to the NH wagging vibration and secondary amine vibration, respectively. The bands at 1029, 1122 and 932 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si [48]. These findings indicate there are abundant –NH2 and –OH on the surface of SiQDs, which endows SiQDs with good water solubility and facilitates their combination with Cu2-β-CD.

The morphologies of the prepared SiQDs and SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters were observed by TEM and shown in Fig. 1 . It is clearly shown that the diameter of as-obtained SiQD is about 6 nm (Fig. 1a). Our previous studies have proved that Cu2-β-CD can coordinate with –NH2 [49,50]. The TEM image in Fig. 1b shows the diameter of the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanocluster is about 100 nm and much larger than that of mere SiQD, which was due to the coordination of Cu2-β-CD on the surface of SiQDs.

The remarkable optical properties of the prepared SiQDs were performed by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S2a). It can be seen that there is a prominent characteristic peak centered at 270 nm along with a shoulder at 360 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peak at 270 nm could be probably assigned to the presence of π-π* transition of the CC bond on the surface of SiQDs [51]. The shoulder peak at 360 nm may be due to n-π* transition and the contribution of the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can lead to strong fluorescence. The fluorescent spectrum for the obtained SiQDs is consistent with the UV–vis absorption features. When excited at 360 nm, SiQDs show very strong fluorescent emission centered at 440 nm, as shown in Fig. S2a. Moreover, the fluorescence is sensitive to the excitation wavelength (Fig. S2b), and with the increase of excitation wavelength, a redshift of emission wavelength happens, after coordinating with Cu2-β-CD, the fluorescence of SiQDs solution decreases (Fig. S2c). This excitation-dependent emission property of SiQDs has been also reported in previous literature [52]. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared SiQDs is calculated to be 23.5 % using quinine sulfate as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters to UA
ce:section.ce:para: SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters are mainly formed via coordination-drivenself- assembly. When the external conditions change, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters may disintegrate or reassemble, resulting in the change of fluorescence intensity of the system. l-Cys and UA had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs whether pH was 8, 10 or 13 (Fig. S2d). At pH 8, l-Cys could promote the fluorescence enhancement of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters, which was 35 % higher than that of original solution, however, UA did not change the fluorescence intensity of the solution, which is consistent with the results in the literature [42] (Fig. 2 a). We speculated this was because of the stronger coordination affinity between -SH of l-Cys and Cu2-β-CD, l-Cys made SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters partly disassemble, which increased the number of free SiQDs in the solution. Because UA could not replace Cu2-β-CD from SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at this pH, the fluorescence intensity of solution remained unchanged after adding UA. At pH 13, the results were very different, UA made the fluorescence intensity of the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters decrease by 30 %, but l-Cys had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the solution. When l-Cys or UA was added at pH 10, the fluorescence intensity increased by 17 % (l-Cys) or decreased by 50 % (UA) (Fig. 2a).

In order to investigate mutual interference between l-Cys and UA in the process of fluorescence detection, UA and the mixture of l-Cys and UA were added into the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters solution at pH 13, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, the alone presence of UA and coexistence with l-Cys had almost the same effect on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters solution, which indicated that l-Cys had not interfere with the detection of UA. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters solution at pH 8 in the alone presence of l-Cys was similar as that of coexistence with UA (Fig. S2e), which showed UA had no interference with the detection of l-Cys. Therefore, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters can be utilized as a fluorescent probe for the detection of UA or l-Cys in the present of another biomolecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence attenuation mechanism of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters in the present of UA at pH = 13
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further clarify fluorescence attenuation mechanism after adding UA at pH 13, the fluorescence change of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters with time was measured. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters increased slightly at the beginning of adding UA and then decreases slowly. After 200 min, the fluorescence intensity tended to be stable, but it was still 30 % lower than the initial value. At this pH, UA firstly ionized to form sodium urate, then slowly converted to allantoin and sodium allantoate [53] (Fig. 3b), which was confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra of UA at different pH (Fig. S3). The anion urate had electrostatic interaction with Cu (ΙΙ) of Cu2-β-CD, which resulted in partial disintegration of the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters and the increase of the fluorescence intensity. Our previous researches have confirmed that high-energy water molecules confined inside the cavity of Cu2-β-CD had electrostatic interaction with Cu (ΙΙ) [49,50]. Because allantoin could enter the cavity of β-CD [54,55] (Fig. 3b) and form H-bonds with high-energy water molecules, it reduced the energy of high-energy water molecules and improved the activity of Cu (ΙΙ) of Cu2-β-CD, which led to form tighter SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters and the further decrease of fluorescence intensity. At this pH, -SH of l-Cys would ionize to anion -S-, which reduced the coordination ability between l-Cys and Cu (ΙΙ), and l-Cys could not enter the cavity of β-CD. Therefore, adding l-Cys did not obviously change the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters (Fig. 2a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of UA based on SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at pH 13
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of UA could be realized by the quantitative relationship between the fluorescence attenuation intensity and the UA concentration. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters decreased gradually with the increase of UA concentration. The relationship between UA concentration and the change of fluorescence intensity satisfied the linear regression equation (I0-I)/I0 = 0.79526CUA + 0.00043 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9984 in the range of 0.025–0.15 mM (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 4.9*10-3 mM based on three times the standard deviation rule. The comparison between linear ranges and LOD of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters and some previously reported fluorescent probes for UA detection demonstrates that our method is comparable to or better than most of them (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters for UA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the present probe, various possibly interfering species including anions/cation ions, urea (UR), amino acids, aspirin (AS), salicylic acid (SA), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and glutathione (Glu) were added into the fluorescence detection system of UA, respectively, and the fluorescent intensity changes were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 , the change of fluorescent intensity was negligible in the present of above interfering species except DA. Although DA significantly interferes with UA detection, the normal concentration of DA (1.3–2.6 μM) [56,57] is much lower than that of UA (1.49–4.46 mM) in urine [31] and will be further decreased with dilution of samples in pretreatment step. Therefore, it would not interfere with UA detection in urine. These results indicated that the fluorescentprobes based on SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters had good specificity and were not disturbed easily by the coexistence of other interfering species.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the developed method in the applicability of real samples, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters were used to detect UA in human urine and l-Cys in milk, respectively. The fresh urine was diluted by 100-fold using NaOH solution with pH = 13. The fresh milk was diluted by 500-fold with deionized water. The obtained results are shown in Table 2 and Table S2. From Table 2, the recoveries of spiked UA standard solutions in urine sample were in the range of 96.5 %-108.0 % with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) ranged from 1.0 to 1.6 %. The obtained recoveries of spiked l-Cys standard solutions in milk sample ranged from 100.0 % to 117.3 % and RSD (n = 3) was less than 1.5 % (Table S2). These results indicated that SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters are precise and reliable for the detection of UA in human urine and l-Cys in milk.

In this work, SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters were prepared by coordination-drivenself-assemblybetweenCu (ΙΙ) onthe Cu(OH–)(O2 –)Cu bridge of Cu2-β-CD and –NH2 on the surface of SiQDs. UA could decrease the fluorescence intensity of nanoclusters at pH 13 via improving the Cu (ΙΙ) activity of Cu2-β-CD caused by the inclusion of Cu2-β-CD with allantoin, decomposed from UA. Nevertheless, l-Cys can enhance the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD nanoclusters at pH 8 by partly disassembling nanoclusters. The change of SiQDs/Cu2-β-CD fluorescence intensity showed a linear response to UA and l-Cys concentration, which was used as a novel fluorescence probe for the detection of UA and l-Cys. More importantly, l-Cys and UA did not interfere with each other's detection. This work might open up a new avenue for the construction of QDs-based fluorescence probe for the detection of UA and l-Cys in the present of another interfering biomolecule.

Yonggang Peng: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Fan Shao: Data curation. Kaixi Guo: Validation. Hanhan Zhuo: Validation. Yuan Wang: Methodology, Supervision. Xiaowei Xie: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yongxin Tao: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775013), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110) and Research Program for the TransformationofScientific andTechnologicalachievements of Huaian City (HA202114).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109765.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric nanosensor for detection of uric acid based on N, P co-doped carbon dots and in-situ formation of Au/Ag core-shell nanoparticles
Microchem. J.

Microchim. Acta

Anal. Methods

J. Chromatogr. B

Anal. Chem.
